title: §164.528 accounting-of-disclosures — retention and responsiveness
exclusions: Does NOT substitute for the full regulatory control set; this UC provides one of several evidence streams and must be combined with policy, procedure, and supplemental UCs to demonstrate the complete clause.
evidence: Scheduled search output persisted to the compliance summary index with regulation=HIPAA Privacy, clause=§164.528, available as a saved search and exportable as a CSV evidence pack for the responsible officer.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Phase-C authored UC targeting HIPAA Privacy §164.528 (Accounting of disclosures). Uses continuous, production-grade Splunk telemetry to provide auditor-facing evidence; assurance level set to 'contributing' pending SME review per Phase E of the regulation-coverage plan.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Privacy §164.528 (Accounting of disclosures) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.50.16: §164.528 accounting-of-disclosures — retention and responsiveness.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.50.16' running on PHI disclosure ledger, patient-request queue logs., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.
compliance.smeCaveat: Phase-C draft: SME review required before uplifting assurance from 'contributing' to 'partial' or 'full'. Confirm that the SPL captures the specific regulator expectation and that the telemetry exists in the deployment.

dataSources: PHI disclosure ledger, patient-request queue logs.
spl:
index=phi_disclosures earliest=-365d
| eval disclosure_age_days=round((now()-strptime(disclosure_date,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))/86400,0)
| stats count AS total_disclosures count(eval(request_type="patient_request")) AS patient_requests count(eval(response_time_days <= 60)) AS on_time BY covered_entity
| eval response_rate=round(100*on_time/patient_requests,1)
| where (total_disclosures=0) OR (patient_requests>0 AND response_rate < 100)
description: Measures accounting-of-disclosures activity and patient-request response rate against the 60-day HIPAA statutory window.
value: §164.528 requires the ability to produce an accounting of disclosures within 60 days; continuous metrics prevent nasty surprises during a HIPAA audit.
implementation: (1) Persist disclosures in a dedicated ledger index; (2) emit patient-request events with response_time_days; (3) schedule monthly. Indexes required: `index=phi_disclosures`.
visualization: Table of flagged records for investigation; single-value KPI of coverage / compliance percentage; time chart of trend over the last 90 days; drill-down per responsible owner. Key fields: `covered_entity`, `disclosure_age_days`, `on_time`, `patient_requests`, `response_rate`, `total_disclosures`.
knownFalsePositives: Tuning prerequisites: source field mappings must be in place before the UC will produce reliable metrics; early runs will commonly flag unmapped systems until onboarding is completed.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for measures accounting-of-disclosures activity and patient-request response rate against the 60-day hipaa statutory window. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: PHI disclosure ledger, patient-request queue logs..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Persist disclosures in a dedicated ledger index; (2) emit patient-request events with response_time_days; (3) schedule monthly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=phi_disclosures earliest=-365d
| eval disclosure_age_days=round((now()-strptime(disclosure_date,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))/86400,0)
| stats count AS total_disclosures count(eval(request_type="patient_request")) AS patient_requests count(eval(response_time_days <= 60)) AS on_time BY covered_entity
| eval response_rate=round(100*on_time/patient_requests,1)
| where (total_disclosures=0) OR (patient_requests>0 AND response_rate < 100)
```

Understanding this SPL

**§164.528 accounting-of-disclosures — retention and responsiveness** — §164.528 requires the ability to produce an accounting of disclosures within 60 days; continuous metrics prevent nasty surprises during a HIPAA audit.

Documented **Data sources**: PHI disclosure ledger, patient-request queue logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: phi_disclosures.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=phi_disclosures, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **disclosure_age_days** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by covered_entity** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **response_rate** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where (total_disclosures=0) OR (patient_requests>0 AND response_rate < 100)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table of flagged records for investigation; single-value KPI of coverage / compliance percentage; time chart of trend over the last 90 days; drill-down per responsible owner.
